text:
Epilepsy Centre 

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 8 April 2013 

Dr Patel  
North London Neurosciences Centre  
Neurology Department, 14 Highgate Hill, London N19 5LW  

Dear Dr Patel,  

Michael Harris, DOB: 28-02-1981, Hospital No: K593024 NHS No. 7482103659  
3A Wren Court, Holloway Road, London N7 6RT  

Diagnoses: 

Focal epilepsy, likely frontal onset, with nocturnal predominance.

I reviewed Michael Harris in our epilepsy clinic today by scheduled telephone appointment, which he prefers given his frequent international business travel and irregular sleep across time zones. He reports generally good adherence to lamotrigine, with occasional delayed evening doses when flying long-haul. He has been using a mobile app to track time zone changes and dosing times, which appears to have reduced missed doses. He denies adverse effects beyond occasional transient dizziness when sleep-deprived. There is no current use of rescue medication and he has not required emergency services since last year.

Seizure history since the last review: In November he had a run of one seizure within half an hour (not full generalised tonic-clonic, fluctuating in intensity, resolved without medication). In December there was 7 further seizures at night, and in March another during physiotherapy (unwitnessed by mother). He associates the November and December events with multiple red-eye flights, jet lag and fragmented sleep. The March episode followed a strenuous rehabilitation session for a minor ankle injury while he was mildly sleep-deprived; there was no reported trauma, tongue biting or incontinence. Post-ictal confusion, when present, is brief. He continues to describe a reliable focal aware aura of a rising epigastric sensation and brief speech arrest, sometimes progressing to impaired awareness; secondary generalisation has not been reported during this interval.

Examination is not applicable today as this was a telephone consultation. Review of prior investigations: MRI brain (last year) was structurally normal; routine EEG showed intermittent right frontal sharp activity with increased expression during drowsiness. No changes to his cardiac or metabolic profiles have been noted by his GP in recent blood tests. 

Plan: 
- Continue lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily. We discussed setting dual time-zone alarms to anchor dosing to home time for flights exceeding 6 hours, shifting by 1–2 hours per day to avoid large dosing gaps. 
- Provide a written travel seizure plan for airlines and hotel staff; patient to carry a copy on all trips. 
- Sleep hygiene advice emphasised, particularly avoidance of red-eye flights where feasible and limiting overnight work before major time zone changes. 
- No rescue medication indicated at present given self-limited events and absence of generalised tonic–clonic seizures; we will revisit if frequency increases. 
- Arrange ambulatory EEG if nocturnal frequency persists over the next quarter. 
- Follow-up in three months by telephone, or earlier if there is escalation, injury, or emergence of generalised tonic–clonic seizures.

Yours sincerely

In November he had a run of one seizure within half an hour (not full generalised tonic-clonic, fluctuating in intensity, resolved without medication). In December there was 7 further seizures at night, and in March another during physiotherapy (unwitnessed by mother)

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: We need to determine current seizure frequency from the letter. The letter provides a detailed seizure history since the last review: 'In November he had a run of one seizure within half an hour... In December there was 7 further seizures at night, and in March another...' This totals 1 (November) + 7 (December) + 1 (March) = 9 seizures across November, December, and March. The clinic date is 8 April 2013, and these events are within a specific timeframe since last review, but only explicit months given are Nov, Dec, and March, which span 3 months with seizures (January and February not mentioned). For normalization, we should convert to a rate over the span described. The clearest explicit period encompassing the listed events is November through March inclusive, which is 5 months (Nov, Dec, Jan, Feb, Mar). Total seizures = 9 over 5 months. No evidence of seizure freedom for 6 months. No higher-frequency alternative is given. Therefore label as '9 per 5 month'. Quote the sentence listing the counts.
seizure_frequency_number: In November he had a run of one seizure within half an hour... In December there was 7 further seizures at night, and in March another
reference: In November he had a run of one seizure within half an hour (not full generalised tonic-clonic, fluctuating in intensity, resolved without medication). In December there was 7 further seizures at night, and in March another during physiotherapy (unwitnessed by mother)
